facts.title: The Korean Wave
facts.fact: The "Korean Wave" (Hallyu) has carried K-pop, dramas, and film to a massive global audience since the late 1990s.
facts.korean_en: I listen to K-pop often.
facts.note: **한류** (Hallyu) literally means "Korean flow/wave."

facts.title: Korea invented the webtoon
facts.fact: Korea pioneered the webtoon — vertical-scroll digital comics made for phones. Many hit dramas and films are now adapted from popular webtoons.
facts.korean_en: I read webtoons every day.
facts.note: **웹툰** = "web" + 툰 (from "cartoon").

facts.title: Squid Game, a global phenomenon
facts.fact: Korean dramas (K-dramas) reach huge audiences worldwide. The survival series **"Squid Game"** (오징어 게임) became **Netflix's biggest series launch ever** in 2021 and won several Emmy Awards.
facts.korean_en: I watch Korean dramas often.
facts.note: **오징어 게임** means "squid game," named after a children's playground game shown in the series.

facts.title: Yeneung, Korean variety shows
facts.fact: **Yeneung** (예능), Korea's variety and entertainment shows, are a huge part of TV. They mix games, travel, and comedy, and often pair celebrities with on-screen captions and sound effects for laughs.
facts.korean_en: Variety shows are really fun.
facts.note: **예능** programs are famous for funny on-screen captions called 자막.

facts.title: K-pop fandom culture
facts.fact: K-pop fandoms are highly organized, and most groups have an **official fan name** — BTS fans are called **ARMY**. Fans learn synchronized chants (**응원**) and even buy subway ads to celebrate idols' birthdays.
facts.korean_en: We cheer at the concert.
facts.note: **응원을 하다** means "to cheer" — fans chant members' names in time with the song.

facts.title: Korea, an esports powerhouse
facts.fact: Korea is a global leader in **esports** (competitive video gaming), with pro players treated like sports stars. Much of the scene grew out of **PC방** (PC bang), internet cafés where people play games by the hour.
facts.korean_en: My friends play games at the PC café.
facts.note: **방** means "room," so a 피시방 is literally a "PC room." The same 방 appears in 노래방 (karaoke room).

facts.title: K-beauty and skincare
facts.fact: **K-beauty** — Korean cosmetics and skincare — is popular around the world. Korea is especially known for elaborate **multi-step skincare routines** and innovative products like sheet masks and cushion compacts.
facts.korean_en: Korean cosmetics are very famous.
facts.note: **화장품** combines 화장 ("makeup") and 품 ("goods, product").

facts.title: Korea's café culture
facts.fact: Korea has an enormous **café culture**, with a remarkably high number of coffee shops per person. Cafés are popular spots to meet friends, study, or work, and many have elaborate themes or desserts.
facts.korean_en: We drink coffee at the café.
facts.note: Many students study at a café or a **스터디 카페** (study café) rented by the hour.

facts.title: KakaoTalk runs daily life
facts.fact: Almost everyone in Korea uses **KakaoTalk** (카카오톡), the dominant messaging app. Its cartoon characters, the **Kakao Friends**, are so popular that they appear on everything from plush toys to bank cards.
facts.korean_en: I send a KakaoTalk message to my friend.
facts.note: People shorten it to **카톡** and use it as a verb: 카톡 해요 = "text me on KakaoTalk." The particle **에게** marks the person receiving something.

facts.title: K-pop conquers the global charts
facts.fact: K-pop groups like **BTS** (방탄소년단) and **BLACKPINK** (블랙핑크) have topped charts worldwide. BTS became the first Korean act to reach **No. 1 on the US Billboard Hot 100**, and their fans helped fill stadiums across the globe.
facts.korean_en: My favorite singers are BTS.
facts.note: **제일** (or 가장) means "most." The full Korean name **방탄소년단** means "Bulletproof Boy Scouts."

facts.title: K-pop idols at global festivals
facts.fact: K-pop has become a fixture at major Western music festivals. **BLACKPINK** headlined the famous **Coachella** festival in the United States in 2023, the first K-pop act to top that bill.
facts.korean_en: That singer performed in the United States.
facts.note: **공연을 하다** means "to give a performance." The particle **에서** marks the place where an action happens.

facts.title: K-pop's 'generations' of idols
facts.fact: Fans group K-pop idol bands into **generations** by era. Groups that debuted in the early 2000s are the "first generation," while today's global stars belong to the **fourth generation** or later.
facts.korean_en: This idol group debuted two years ago.
facts.note: **데뷔** comes from the French/English "debut." **전** after a time word means "ago": 2년 전 = "two years ago."

facts.title: Photocards, the must-have collectible
facts.fact: K-pop albums come with random **photocards** (포토카드) of group members, and fans trade them eagerly to collect their favorite "bias." Rare cards can be swapped or sold for surprisingly high prices.
facts.korean_en: I collect photocards of the singer I like.
facts.note: Fans often shorten 포토카드 to **포카**. The verb **모으다** is used for collecting things.

facts.title: Lightsticks light up concerts
facts.fact: Each major K-pop group has its own official **lightstick** (응원봉), with a unique shape and color. At concerts, thousands of synced lightsticks turn the audience into a glowing "ocean" of the group's color.
facts.korean_en: I wave a lightstick at the concert.
facts.note: **응원봉** combines 응원 ("cheering") and 봉 ("stick, rod"). Each fandom's color creates a unique "sea" at shows.

facts.title: Mukbang, eating broadcasts
facts.fact: **Mukbang** (먹방) — videos of a host eating large amounts of food while chatting with viewers — started in Korea and became a worldwide online trend. The word now appears in many languages.
facts.korean_en: I watch mukbang before going to sleep.
facts.note: **먹방** is a blend of 먹다 ("to eat") and 방송 ("broadcast"). The ending **-기 전에** means "before doing": 자기 전에 = "before sleeping."

facts.title: Aegyo, acting cute
facts.fact: **Aegyo** (애교) is the art of acting adorably cute through a sweet voice, gestures, and facial expressions. Idols are often asked to "show some aegyo" on variety shows, to fans' delight.
facts.korean_en: That person has a lot of aegyo (is very cute/charming).
facts.note: People say **애교가 많다** ("to have a lot of aegyo") to describe someone who acts cute and affectionate.

facts.title: The MBTI craze
facts.fact: Korea is famous for its **MBTI** obsession — the 16-type personality test. Young people often ask each other's MBTI type early on, and idols frequently share theirs with fans.
facts.korean_en: What is your MBTI?
facts.note: **엠비티아이** is how MBTI is written in Korean letters. 성격 유형 means "personality type."

facts.title: Character merch everywhere
facts.fact: Cute **characters** are huge business in Korea. **Line Friends** (such as Brown the bear) and **Kakao Friends** (like Ryan the lion) appear on plush toys, stationery, phone cases, and even themed cafés and stores.
facts.korean_en: I really like the teddy bear (bear doll).
facts.note: **인형** means "doll" or "plush toy." Brown from Line Friends is a 곰 ("bear").

facts.title: 'Bballi bballi' culture
facts.fact: Koreans are known for **"bballi bballi"** (빨리빨리) culture — a fast-paced "hurry, hurry" attitude. It shows up in famously quick food delivery, fast internet, and a general love of speed and efficiency.
facts.korean_en: Let's go quickly!
facts.note: Repeating **빨리** as 빨리빨리 stresses urgency. This phrase is one of the first Korean expressions many visitors learn.

facts.title: Emoticons in chat
facts.fact: In Korean chat apps, paid cartoon stickers called **emoticons** (이모티콘) are wildly popular for expressing feelings. People also type **ㅋㅋㅋ** to mean laughter, like "haha," since ㅋ stands for the "k" laughing sound.
facts.korean_en: I sent an emoticon to my friend.
facts.note: **ㅋㅋㅋ** (kkk) is the Korean equivalent of "hahaha." Sticker-style **이모티콘** are often bought in sets.

facts.title: Couple looks and 100-day dates
facts.fact: Korean couples often wear matching **couple outfits** (커플룩) and celebrate relationship milestones counted in days — the **100th day** (백일) together is an especially big one.
facts.korean_en: Today is our 100th day (together).
facts.note: **백일** (100 days) uses Sino-Korean numbers: 백 = 100. The same word 백일 also names a baby's 100th-day celebration.

facts.title: Dating reality shows
facts.fact: Korean **dating reality shows** have become global hits on streaming services. Programs that follow ordinary singles looking for love — including some with surprising twists — attract big international audiences.
facts.korean: 이 연애 프로그램이 아주 인기가 많아요.
facts.korean_en: This dating show is very popular.
facts.note: **인기가 많다** literally "to have much popularity" = "to be popular." 연애 means romantic dating.

facts.title: A nation of mobile gamers
facts.fact: Korea is one of the world's biggest **mobile gaming** markets. Riding on super-fast internet and smartphones, mobile games are a hugely popular way for people of all ages to play on the go.
facts.korean_en: I play mobile games on the subway.
facts.note: **휴대폰** means "mobile phone" (휴대 = "to carry"); people also say 핸드폰 or 폰.
